title: Jaya, Who Rules the State, Would Do Well to Restrain Her Tongue!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source confirms `மிக உயர்` (supercritical/high-efficiency), the date 29 March 2012, and the employment figure 3.78 lakh women, which is badly damaged in OCR. It prints the chimney measure as `275 உயர` without an explicit unit; the translation renders the technically intended height as 275 metres but does not alter the canonical source. The duplicated title fragment and only source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR/page-break errors are repaired in the Tamil reading copy. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Speaking at election campaign meetings in Tiruppur and Kangeyam, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa held forth at length on electricity generation in Tamil Nadu. She said: "It is amusing that DMK president Karunanidhi, who left every power project in ruin while his party was in office at both the Centre and the State, now asks about the fate of projects being implemented vigorously. In the coming Lok Sabha election, the DMK, which showed no initiative in taking steps to provide the electricity Tamil Nadu needs, must be dealt an unforgettable sledgehammer blow." Newspapers such as Dina Thanthi and Dinamani displayed this prominently.
During that speech, Jayalalithaa also replied to an accusation I had raised on 3 March 2014.

Full-page advertisements appeared in every newspaper, correction, in every newspaper supporting the AIADMK, about Jayalalithaa laying the foundation stone for the 660 MW Ennore Expansion Supercritical Thermal Power Station Project, supposedly intended to make Tamil Nadu a power-surplus State. I had asked: "In a long statement read under Rule 110 on 29 March 2012, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced that a new 660 MW Ennore Thermal Power Expansion Project would be launched at an estimated cost of ₹3,960 crore and begin generation by the end of 2015. The Union Environment Ministry granted clearance on 24 January 2013 and the Pollution Control Board on 18 February 2013, a year ago. If the Chief Minister is only now laying the foundation stone, does this not reveal the urgency and concern the AIADMK government shows towards electricity generation?

"Electricity Board sources say the foundation was delayed for a year because of a price dispute between the company selected to implement the project and the Tamil Nadu government. If laying the foundation stone for a scheme announced in the Assembly in March 2012 itself takes two years, how many more years will it take for the project to generate electricity? Is this the speed with which the AIADMK government increases generation and removes power cuts? Was it fair to spend taxpayers' money on full-page newspaper advertisements for laying, by video conference, the foundation stone of a project already gravely delayed at its very outset?" Imagining she was answering these questions at Tiruppur, she merely plastered over them.

Her reply offered no explanation for giving full-page advertisements only to pro-AIADMK newspapers. Explaining the two-year delay, Jayalalithaa said Union environmental clearance was received on 24 January 2013, tenders were then examined, Lanco Infratech was finally selected, and she issued the work order to it on 27 February 2014. That was precisely what I had explained.

I had asked why thirteen months were needed to finalise the tender even after Union clearance arrived on 24 January 2013. Since Amma had no acceptable answer for this avoidable delay in an important project, she simply left the question unanswered.

Jayalalithaa said in her speech: "The 660 MW Ennore Expansion Supercritical Thermal Power Station Project was announced by me in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 29 March 2012." But even before she announced it in the House, the DMK government had obtained Airports Authority of India clearance on 16 May 2008 for the project's 275-metre-high chimney, and Coastal Regulation Zone clearance on 23 December 2008. Concealing this truth both from the Assembly and the people, she speaks as though she newly announced the project herself.

Writing in Murasoli on 3 March 2014 about the new Ennore project, I asked what had happened to the other generation schemes the Chief Minister announced when she presented this one under Rule 110 in March 2012. I also asked about the projects already announced in the 2011–12 Budget: an 800 MW Udangudi expansion costing ₹4,800 crore; a 1,600 MW Uppur thermal project costing ₹9,600 crore; a new 600 MW generating unit costing ₹3,600 crore to replace the forty-year-old Ennore Thermal Power Station; and the 800 MW fourth-stage Thoothukudi project costing ₹4,800 crore. It was announced that these government and joint-venture projects would invest a total of ₹22,800 crore and add 3,800 MW of generation. What became of all of them? Chief Minister Jayalalithaa offered no explanation because she had no answer.

I had also asked: "After the AIADMK assumed office, how many projects became operational and how much electricity is being generated? Before leaving to campaign tomorrow, will the Chief Minister give the country figures?" Perhaps nothing was written for her about these schemes, so she did not open her mouth about them. Instead, she angrily said I had left every power project in ruin, shown no initiative in providing the needed electricity, and therefore deserved an unforgettable "sledgehammer blow."

What initiative did the DMK government actually show in providing the required electricity? What effort did Jayalalithaa make under AIADMK rule? I have explained this many times with statistics. Here it is once again.

Although memoranda of understanding for ten generation projects were signed while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister under AIADMK rule from 1991 to 1996, no necessary land was acquired and no environmental clearance obtained for any project. In 2010, thirteen generation works were under way under the DMK government: North Chennai Stage II; Mettur; Thoothukudi; Vallur; Bhavani Kattalai II and III; Bhavani Barrage I and II; Periyar-Vaigai I, II, III and IV; and Udangudi.

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced seven projects in the Assembly as about to begin generation: Mettur; Vallur Units I, II and III; North Chennai Units I and II; and two units at Thoothukudi. Every one of these projects was designed and its preliminary work undertaken during DMK rule. Electricity from projects initiated by that government is what now gives Tamil Nadu substantial relief in a period of crisis.

During Jayalalithaa's 2001–06 government, only ₹700 crore in total was spent on electricity generation. Yet at Tiruppur, Amma accused me of showing no initiative while I was Chief Minister from 2006 to 2011. During those five years, expenditure on generation was ₹774 crore in 2007–08; ₹710 crore in 2008–09; ₹2,800 crore in 2009–10; ₹3,100 crore in 2010–11; and ₹3,700 crore in 2011–12, totalling ₹11,084 crore.

The DMK government approved ₹3,700 crore for generation expenditure in 2011–12. Do you know how much Jayalalithaa's government approved the following year, 2012–13? ₹2,067 crore, about ₹1,700 crore less than the DMK allocation. And in this splendid condition, she makes the fraudulent claim that I showed no initiative!

In the AIADMK election manifesto, Jayalalithaa said 1,000 MW would be generated from waste in 151 municipalities and corporations by 2012. After three years in office, has even preparatory work begun? I have asked this many times. Why has there still been no answer?

On 20 October 2012, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced a Solar Energy Policy in the Assembly and said 3,000 MW would be generated over three years. Will she say how many megawatts have so far been produced through that policy?

At Tiruppur, Jayalalithaa boasted that "electricity generation and industrial development in Tamil Nadu continue to grow." I have already pointed out the condition of industrial development, how investments are moving to other States, and how Karnataka's Chief Minister came to Coimbatore itself to hold a conference attracting investors. Here is another example of industrial growth under this government.

Under the DMK government, 4.93 lakh women worked in factories. Under the present AIADMK government, the figure is 3.78 lakh. The number of women who lost employment under AIADMK rule is 1.15 lakh. Is this the mark of industrial growth?

Women who enjoyed employment under the DMK government have lost it and suffer under Jayalalithaa's rule. Where is industrial development shining in Tamil Nadu? How grotesquely amusing that a woman Chief Minister unable to protect even the jobs already held by lakhs of women should claim that industry is growing. Is speaking with the tongue and having newspapers print and advertise it enough? Should it not be implemented in the country?

Another example: the Tamil Nadu government operates cement factories at Alangulam and Ariyalur. At Alangulam, production under the DMK government in 2010–11 was 2,03,040 tonnes. Under AIADMK rule in 2011–12, it was 1,34,640 tonnes, a fall of 68,400 tonnes. At the Ariyalur government cement factory, production was 4,90,035 tonnes under the DMK and 3,88,055 tonnes under the AIADMK, a decline of 1,01,980 tonnes. Is this the sign that industrial development has increased in Tamil Nadu? Will the Chief Minister answer all this in detail?

Udanpirappē, whether Amma answers or not, you must take these facts to the people and explain them. Make clear what kind of government rules Tamil Nadu, how it uses empty announcements and futile fanfare to divert people, and how it attempts to deceive them. I know well that you continue to perform this work. Even so, is it not my duty to assemble the necessary facts and help you? That is what I am doing, confident that you will advance further and further in your work. Is not our obligation to labour every day for the Tamil people?
With affection,
M. K.
16 March 2014
